name: Bill Doggett
description: American jazz and rhythm and blues pianist and organist (1916-1996)
abstract: William Ballard Doggett (February 16, 1916 – November 13, 1996) was an American pianist and organist. He began his career playing swing music before transitioning into rhythm and blues.

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Liebman
about_quotes.evidence: Honky Tonk, Bill Doggett. They were r-and-b guys who snuck into the pop charts, and I heard – that’s where I heard tenor.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Liebman — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Al Hall
about_quotes.evidence: I had been working with Bill Doggett in Philadelphia and we had a big band

about_quotes.speaker: Ram Ramirez
about_quotes.evidence: Well, when I heard him, he was great. I loved the way he played it. ... So Mr. Wells, eventually he said, 'You know, you and Marlowe Morris and the rest of the gang, and Bill Doggett, owe me an organ, because you learned on my organ!'

about_quotes.speaker: James Hunter
about_quotes.evidence: a good example is somebody like Bill Doggett, whose contributions to his own records aren’t always readily sort of apparent but he’s there driving it from underneath with the bass pedals making it sort of throb along.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Hunter interviewed by Monk Rowe, Boston, Massachusetts, July 25, 2018

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: The top instrumental of all time at that time, or one of the tops – maybe the tops at the time was Honky Tonk, Bill Doggett’s group. So I learned to play every lick in it, all the guitar licks.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: I learned in the chitlin circuits. That’s where I learned. You know, with Wild Bill Davis and Bill Doggett and I learned as children.
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Row, via Zoom, July 21, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]
